questions.stem: Which task is most appropriate for the nurse to delegate to an assistive personnel (AP)?

options.text: Measuring and recording a stable patient's vital signs
Teaching a patient about a new medication
Assessing chest pain
Evaluating a new wound drainage change

questions.rationale: Vital signs on a stable patient are a routine, predictable task that can be delegated when the nurse retains responsibility for follow-up. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Teaching requires nursing judgment. - C Assessment of chest pain must be done by the nurse. - D Wound changes need nursing assessment.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it uses teaching before the physiologic risk is controlled. "Measuring and recording a stable patient's vital signs" fits the safer response to **Why the others are wrong:** - B Teaching requires nursing judgment.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Assessing chest pain" moves care toward routine care before infection or sepsis escalation.
distractorRationales.d: "Evaluating a new wound drainage change" misses the bedside risk because - C Assessment of chest pain must be done by the nurse points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.

questions.takeaway: Delegate routine data collection, not assessment or teaching.

questions.stem: A patient suddenly says, "I can't catch my breath," and is leaning forward. What should the nurse do first?

options.text: Assess airway, breathing, and oxygenation immediately
Tell the patient to lie flat and relax
Wait to see if the feeling passes
Focus on obtaining a full history first

questions.rationale: Sudden dyspnea can signal acute respiratory distress. The nurse should assess breathing and oxygenation before anything less urgent. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Lying flat can worsen breathing for some patients. - C Delay can miss a serious problem. - D A focused assessment comes before a long history.

distractorRationales.b: Because the nurse should assess breathing and oxygenation before anything less urgent suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Tell the patient to lie flat and relax" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Assess airway, breathing, and oxygenation immediately" fits the safer response to sudden dyspnea can signal acute respiratory distress.
distractorRationales.d: "Focus on obtaining a full history first" misses the bedside risk because the nurse should assess breathing and oxygenation before anything less urgent points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Sudden breathing changes require immediate reassessment.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A patient on oxygen has a falling oxygen saturation trend and is now more short of breath. What is the best nursing concern?

options.text: The respiratory status may be worsening
The patient is improving because oxygen is still on
A single normal reading later will cancel the trend
Breathing changes only matter if the patient is anxious

questions.rationale: A worsening trend matters even if oxygen is already being used. The nurse should reassess the patient and the support. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Oxygen use does not guarantee stability. - C A trend should not be ignored. - D Anxiety is not the only explanation for shortness of breath.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "A patient on oxygen has a falling oxygen saturation trend and is now more short of breath" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: "A single normal reading later will cancel the trend" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "A worsening trend matters even if oxygen is already being used" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Breathing changes only matter if the patient is anxious" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before airway and breathing intervention.

questions.takeaway: Trends often matter more than one isolated number.

visualRationale.conclusion: Trends often matter more than one isolated number.

questions.stem: After surgery, a patient has shallow breathing, a low oxygen saturation, and is reluctant to cough because of pain. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: The patient may be developing postoperative respiratory compromise
The patient is stable because pain is the main issue
The oxygen saturation is not meaningful after surgery
Coughing should be avoided completely

questions.rationale: Pain can limit deep breathing and coughing, which increases the risk of atelectasis and respiratory decline. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Pain and breathing are linked here. - C Oxygen saturation remains important. - D Incentive breathing and cough are often part of recovery.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Pain-related shallow breathing can become a respiratory problem" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "After surgery, a patient has shallow breathing, a low oxygen saturation" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "The patient may be developing postoperative respiratory compromise" fits the safer response to pain-related shallow breathing can become a respiratory problem.

questions.takeaway: Pain-related shallow breathing can become a respiratory problem.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A patient receives a sedating medication and becomes less responsive than before. What should the nurse do first?

options.text: Reassess airway, breathing, and level of consciousness
Assume the sedation is expected and continue the routine
Leave the patient alone until the next scheduled round
Document the change only after the shift ends

questions.rationale: Any decrease in responsiveness after a sedating medication requires immediate reassessment for airway and breathing compromise. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Expected sedation still needs safety checks. - C Delay can miss deterioration. - D Documentation follows assessment, not the other way around.

distractorRationales.b: "Assume the sedation is expected and continue the routine" misses the bedside risk because any decrease in responsiveness after a sedating medication requires immediate reassessment for airway and breathing compromise points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "After sedating meds, responsiveness and breathing must be watched closely" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: Because any decrease in responsiveness after a sedating medication requires immediate reassessment for airway and breathing compromise suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Document the change only after the shift ends" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: After sedating meds, responsiveness and breathing must be watched closely.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A patient suddenly becomes difficult to arouse and is not following commands. What is the nurse's best action?

options.text: Perform a focused neurologic assessment and check for a reversible cause
Assume the patient is just tired
Wait until the next round to reassess
Offer food and fluids first

questions.rationale: An acute change in mental status may reflect stroke, hypoxia, glucose change, medication effect, or another urgent cause. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Sudden reduced responsiveness should not be dismissed. - C Delay can miss a serious decline. - D Food and fluids do not replace a neuro assessment.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Perform a focused neurologic assessment and check for a reversible cause" fits the safer response to **Why the others are wrong:** - B Sudden reduced responsiveness should not be dismissed.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "**Why the others are wrong:** - B Sudden reduced responsiveness should not be dismissed" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "**Why the others are wrong:** - B Sudden reduced responsiveness should not be dismissed" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: New neurologic decline needs prompt assessment, not delay.

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: "The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency" raises concern for clinical safety risk, so bedside safety action is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.
distractorRationales.d: "These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision" raises concern for clinical safety risk, so bedside safety action is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave renal injury untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids" fits the safer response to hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses renal injury; "Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn" moves care toward routine care before renal protection.
distractorRationales.d: "Teach the client about low-sodium foods" uses teaching before the physiologic risk is controlled. The cue "sodium 128 mEq/L" raises concern for renal injury, so renal protection is needed before teaching or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: Because the causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Obtain a full allergy history before intervening" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: Because the causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine" fits the safer response to a client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses neurologic deterioration; "Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature" moves care toward routine care before neurologic safety assessment.
distractorRationales.c: Because severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered suggests neurologic deterioration, "Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes" would not correct the active problem. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses neurologic deterioration; "Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes" moves care toward routine observation before neurologic safety assessment.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.

questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline" fits the safer response to a nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia.
distractorRationales.c: Because chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction suggests circulation or perfusion, "Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward false reassurance instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Chills, low back pain" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: Because c diff spore precautions infection suggests infection progression, "Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Place the client on droplet precautions only" moves care toward routine care before infection or sepsis escalation.
distractorRationales.d: Because c diff spore precautions infection suggests infection progression, "Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.
